title: Beyond Translation-Invariance in Arithmetic Harmonic Analysis
speaker: Trevor Wooley
abstract: Essentially optimal estimates have been obtained for mean values of Vinogradov’s exponential sum as a consequence of the decoupling method (by Bourgain, Demeter and Guth), and the efficient congruencing method (by the speaker). Such work makes essential use of the fact that the system of Diophantine equations associated with these mean values is translation-dilation invariant. We sketch out such ideas, and report on progress for systems which are not translation-dilation invariant obtained by exploiting the arithmetic of function fields over the field of p-adic numbers. Counting problems for Diophantine equations feature prominently.

proof_stages.label: Translation-dilation invariant Vinogradov systems
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines translation-dilation invariance via linear and binomial-expansion examples, introduces the Vinogradov system with equations of degrees 1 through k, and explains by orthogonality that moments of the corresponding exponential sum count its integer solutions.

proof_stages.label: Classical mean-value heuristics and optimal VMVT technology
proof_stages.summary: Quadratic lower-bound heuristics motivate the conjectured mean-value size, after which the speaker reviews the historical Vinogradov mean value theorem, its modern optimal proofs by decoupling and efficient congruencing, and the broader Wronskian/nonvanishing polynomial estimates.

proof_stages.label: New missing-linear-term mean-value theorem
proof_stages.summary: The new result treats monomial exponential sums beginning with n^2 rather than n, a genuinely non-translation-invariant situation for k>2 where previous curvature or decoupling ranges fell short of the conjectured degree-sum range.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary linear equation and shift averaging
proof_stages.summary: For the k=3 square-cube case, the tenth moment is augmented by a compensating linear equation with an auxiliary h, shifted by z, averaged over z, and rewritten as a mixed mean value involving a cubic Vinogradov sum and an auxiliary h,z exponential sum.

proof_stages.label: Enveloping translation-invariant system for the auxiliary moment
proof_stages.summary: The auxiliary sixth moment is embedded in a larger derivative-closed translation-dilation invariant system, and a diagonal specialization of its cubic Vinogradov subsystem converts the optimal upper bound for the enlarged system into the desired optimal bound for the original non-invariant auxiliary moment.

proof_stages.label: Efficient congruencing as p-adic concentration
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sketches efficient congruencing: weighted mean values are concentrated into common p-adic residue classes, translation invariance converts mixed congruences into stronger congruences, and an induction on rank recycles the resulting residue-class refinement.

proof_stages.label: Function-field/nilpotent-thickening interpretation
proof_stages.summary: The enlarged systems are reinterpreted as congruences over a two-dimensional function-field-like quotient with t^2=0, where a one-variable Hensel/Newton refinement using invertible linear derivatives lets efficient congruencing extend to further non-translation-invariant polynomial systems.

examples.name: Quadratic exponential sum over squares
examples.note: The square-sum moment displays the two unavoidable contributions, diagonal solutions and a near-zero local-density contribution, thereby motivating the conjectural mean-value size.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses orthogonality to interpret the quadratic exponential sum as counting solutions and derives lower-bound contributions of about X^s and X^{2s-2}.
